rationale_for_baseline_tier: A new symptomatic superficial thrombophlebitis on a background of varicose veins is usually benign, but the source clinician judged it needed urgent in-person assessment to clinically exclude a concurrent deep vein thrombosis and spreading cellulitis before it could be treated as benign. A telehealth service can do none of the discriminating steps (palpation, calf-circumference comparison, Homan's sign, pedal pulses, venous duplex). The honest telehealth tier is therefore T3 — urgent same-day in-person assessment (GP/urgent care) with duplex — not routine review (which under-triages a potential DVT), and not 000 (no current PE features, massive swelling, or systemic compromise). Escalate to T4/T5 immediately if red flags develop.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rapidly spreading redness up the leg, increasing pain, or fever/feeling unwell
escalation_edges.rationale: Spreading erythema or systemic features suggest cellulitis or extending thrombophlebitis needing in-person review, antibiotics, or emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness spreads quickly up your leg, the pain worsens sharply, or you get a fever or feel unwell, go to an emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden massive swelling of the whole calf or leg, or a new deep aching in the calf muscle
escalation_edges.rationale: These features raise the probability of a deep vein thrombosis, which needs urgent duplex and anticoagulation assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your whole calf suddenly swells up or you get a deep ache in the calf muscle, get an urgent in-person assessment that day — this can signal a deep clot.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden chest pain, shortness of breath, or coughing up blood
escalation_edges.rationale: These are features of pulmonary embolism from a clot travelling to the lungs — an immediate life-threatening emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you suddenly get chest pain, trouble breathing, or cough up blood, call an ambulance on 000 straight away — a clot may have travelled to your lungs.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Pre-existing chronic venous insufficiency and varicose veins provide the substrate for recurrent thrombophlebitis and ongoing venous disease
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Reinforce longer-term venous management (graduated compression once settled, weight and activity, reduce prolonged standing) alongside the acute plan.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Adult (~52, inferred) — no paediatric dosing concerns; standard adult NSAID cautions apply
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm renal/GI risk and any anticoagulant use at the in-person review before continuing the NSAID.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This needs a same-day in-person check because we cannot rule out a deep clot over a video call. See your GP or an urgent care clinic today so they can examine the leg and arrange an ultrasound of the leg veins. In the meantime, take ibuprofen 400 mg three times a day with food, keep the leg elevated above heart level when you can, use a warm compress on the sore area, and stop wearing the tight elastic socks.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Same-day GP appointment or urgent care clinic; request a lower-limb venous duplex ultrasound
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours and no same-day GP or urgent care is available, attend an emergency department for the leg assessment. Call 000 immediately for chest pain or breathlessness.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth-identified new symptomatic superficial thrombophlebitis over pre-existing varicose veins; DVT not excludable remotely. Requesting same-day in-person exam and venous duplex to exclude DVT and assess proximity to the saphenofemoral junction; interim NSAID and conservative measures advised.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassuring the patient that this is benign varicose veins and deferring to self-care or routine GP review.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A concurrent or propagating deep vein thrombosis is missed and extends or embolises to the lungs before any in-person assessment.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Arranging only a prompt (24–48h) GP review rather than a same-day assessment, delaying DVT exclusion.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending the patient to call 000 for an ambulance when there are no PE features, systemic compromise, or massive swelling — safe but a disproportionate use of emergency resources.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether a concurrent deep vein thrombosis is present, and how far the superficial clot extends toward the saphenofemoral junction — neither determinable via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Name the uncertainty honestly, do not reassure the patient it is benign, and arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment with venous duplex while giving clear escalation triggers.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Concluding it is 'just varicose veins', giving reassurance, and deferring to routine review — or, at the other extreme, calling 000 with no emergency features.
